title: newuidmap: write to uid_map failed: Invalid argument
body:
### Issue Description

Failed to build an image, see details below
`newuidmap: write to uid_map failed: Invalid argument`


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Starting a workspace from [1] in DevSpaces on OpenShift
[1] https://gist.githubusercontent.com/tolusha/5f92d30b7456f614cc793fc0c671c28e/raw/604d1c3ec4283d29283a16390cee658b6c982fd1/test.yaml


### Describe the results you received

```
$ podman build .
ERRO[0000] running `/usr/bin/newuidmap 380 0 1000750000 1 1 100000 65536 65537 100000 65536`: newuidmap: write to uid_map failed: Invalid argument 
Error: cannot set up namespace using "/usr/bin/newuidmap": exit status 1
```

### Describe the results you expected

Image successfully built

### podman info output

```yaml
$ podman info
ERRO[0000] running `/usr/bin/newuidmap 476 0 1000750000 1 1 100000 65536 65537 100000 65536`: newuidmap: write to uid_map failed: Invalid argument 
Error: cannot set up namespace using "/usr/bin/newuidmap": exit status 1
```

### Podman in a container

Yes

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

N/A

### Additional information

I think something misconfigured, but I can figure it out.
It happens when OpenShift injects a user into a container.

```
$ podman --version
podman version 5.4.0
```

```
$ rpm -q podman
podman-5.4.0-12.el9_6.x86_64
```

```
$ cat /etc/subuid
1000750000:100000:65536

$ cat /etc/subgid
1000750000:100000:65536

$ id
uid=1000750000(1000750000) gid=0(root) groups=0(root),1000750000

$ cat /etc/passwd | grep 1000750000
1000750000:x:1000750000:0:1000750000 user:/home/user:/sbin/nologin
```

comments.body:
Hey @tolusha 

You mention you are trying to run Podman inside an OpenShift DevSpace, so this is like running Podman in Podman as OpenShift is giving you a container to work in.

Running Podman in Podman is supported and awesome but requires some special setup, if you don't personally control the outer "host" container (the one OpenShift is giving you) then you don't have much hope of getting this to work because by default OpenShift makes their containers nice and secure.

You can see some of the "special setup" discussed here: https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/podman-inside-container

To get this working in your OpenShift environment this looks to be the special setup required: https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2024/12/02/enable-nested-containers-openshift-dev-spaces-user-namespaces#

---
While looking into this issue I noted this as well which I don't think is your root issue but I took time to look into it so I'm leaving it here for discussion. 

I think there is an issue with the uid maps overlaping. If we take a look at the man page: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/newuidmap.1.html and I annotate the command Podman is generating we can see the issue more clearly:

`newuidmap pid_of_podman=380 [user_id_in_namespace=0, user_id_on_host=1000750000, count=1], [user_id_in_namespace=1, user_id_on_host=100000, count=65536], [user_id_in_namespace=65537, user_id_on_host=100000, count=65536]`

- We are setting the uidmap for the user namespace podman is running in
- user 0(root) is being mapped to 1000750000 on the host.
- UID 1 to 65536 inside the namespace are mapped to the host UIDs 100000 to 165536
- UID 65537 to 131073 inside the namespace are mapped to the host UIDs 100000 to 165536

So my suspicion is those last two mappings are conflicting on the host and causing the newuidmap to error. 

The issue is, from what I can see in the Podman code base we are just reading /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid for the current user.

But I don't see the 65537 UID defined in your /etc/subuid, /etc/subgid files...

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

Hello @ninja-quokka,

Thank you for your response.
I reviewed the Podman source code and didn’t find anything suspicious related to the execution of the newuidmap command. So, I built Podman from source and copied it into a container.
Somehow, it magically started working without any issues.

Interestingly, the original Podman installation also began working afterward, and I was able to build an image.

Could you please suggest what I could debug to understand why the originally installed Podman (from the RPM package) was not working?

@tolusha I'm happy to hear the issue has somewhat resolved itself, as for helping you understand why, well that's a little tricky when we didn't really get to the bottom of the issue in the first place.

Do you still have access to the Podman binary distributed via rpm package? `podman version 5.4.0`

If so you could see if the issue is still reproducible with the old Podman, if so you can you [git bisect](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-bisect) to identify what commit fixes the issue. 

If you can no longer reproduce the issue with the old version of Podman, given the issue you were hitting I guess it was something environmental in OpenShift DevSpace.

Hi.

I keep debuging. 
From what I can see, [1] returns 2 identical ranges `{100000 65536}` which causes the issue later ^(


[1] https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/vendor/github.com/containers/storage/pkg/idtools/idtools_supported.go#L59

Basically, if user name equals its id, then the same range will be added twice ^(

[1] https://github.com/shadow-maint/shadow/blob/master/lib/subordinateio.c#L887-L906

I ran into this problem as well, building `toolbox` / Toolbx from source on an Ubuntu LTS system.

At this point, I've already overcome this problem and relieved to be done with it, so my memory is vague on what the specific cause was, but I'll proffer these two things:

* `newuidmap` doesn't exist at the path `podman` (or in my case, `toolbox`) expected to find it
  * `strace -fe status=failed` might help illuminate this
  * I had installed `toolbox` to a non-standard path, and it seemed in my case that it was looking for a non-existent `newuidmap` _there_, instead of `/usr/bin` where the system-provided one already existed; seems odd, but I worked around it by installing shadow-utils into the same directory hierarchy and this helped me get past this problem
* `newuidmap` is not owned by and setuid root
  * in my case, I had to `sudo make install` for shadow-utils to work around this, as I had done `make install` as my regular user, who had read/write permissions to the destination path

Hope that helps somebody. I wouldn't _willingly_ repeat the entire process of building shadow-utils and Toolbx from source (on a new system), but if there is something here that would help narrow down the cause, I'll do it for the team.

Hey! I ran into the same problem and I was just able to fix it.
For me, it was that my file `/etc/subuid` contained duplicated entries for my user. That was caused by something else I was working on, but ended up affecting the normal functioning of podman.
Removing those duplicated entries and rebooting my system fixed the issue for me.
Hope this entry contributes.

I encountered this issue after making a fairly innocent change to /etc/subgid:
```
server:524288:65536
# added these lines:
server:105:1
server:7:1
```
I added server:105:1 to allow the 'server' user to start rootless containers that have the render/printer/whatever group gid mapped inside the user namespace to make images like linuxserver's webtop work.

It worked for that purpose, but with a nasty side effect: podman inside podman stops working entirely:

```
[server@host1 ~]$ podman run -it --rm podman podman info
ERRO[0000] running `/usr/bin/newgidmap 7 0 0 1 1 1 65535`: newgidmap: write to gid_map failed: Operation not permitted 
Error: cannot set up namespace using "/usr/bin/newgidmap": exit status 1
# we don't even need to create a container explicitly to trigger this, seems like podman info already requires creating an inner user namespace (let's call it container 2)
```

The gid map podman makes for container 1 looks like this:

```
[server@host1 ~]$ podman run -it --rm podman cat /proc/self/gid_map
# inner gid,  outer gid, length
         0       1000          1
         1          7          1
         2        105          1
         3     524288      65536
```

The latest LLM made a pretty good explanation of how podman comes up with that but due to the AI policy I don't think I can include the screenshot directly so here's the link to it: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08281788-6ea5-415f-8c30-7332019e4dbe

What about container 2, the inner one that podman tries to create for `podman info`? Well, let's see how /etc/subgid look like in container 1:

```
[server@host1 ~]$ podman run -it --rm podman cat /etc/subgid
root:1:65535
```

Based on that, the podman inside container 1 tries to create a gid mapping with command `newgidmap 7 0 0 1 1 1 65535`, (7 is just the namespace identifier). This means a would-be gid_map like

```
# inner gid, outer gid, length
0 0 1
1 1 65535
```

But creating it fails because the inner gid map entries need to be within a single entry in the outer gid map, and the 1-65535 -> 1-65535 mapping would overlap the `1 7 1`, `2 105 1` and `3 524288 65536` in the outer gid map.

Details and references to documentation, kernel source code and LKML (can't include directly due to AI policy): https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5aa30af5-8ec9-4c18-8ef7-c12203386dff

sSo, there *was* a patch to make this work on kernel-level but it seems to not have been merged https://lkml.indiana.edu/hypermail/linux/kernel/2106.0/05719.html, citing security concerns and that the splitting can and should be done by userspace tools.

Seems like podman has a method for it but it isn't activated in this case.

More information about that: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3ce719c3-a49e-44c3-9d3f-6049ebdca198

I might check if it could be changed to handle this situation too, but also a solution to make things work is changing the /etc/subgid inside the container to be like

```
root:1:1
root:2:1
root:3:65533
```
